description: A bounded thing is 'pheno' when its contents fill the relevant capacity or give its visible form a rounded fullness. This is a degree of filling; 'sholu' (complete) instead says that every required part or step is present. Whether the contents meet a need is the separate judgement 'henoi' (ENOUGH).
articulatory_notes: The lips narrow without closing for voiceless bilabial 'ph' and release into true-mid front 'e'. The tongue tip then contacts the upper teeth for dental nasal 'n', with voice resonating through the nose into rounded mid-back 'o'. Stress falls on the first syllable.
sound_symbolism: The airy opening closes into the nasal centre of 'pheno' before the lips round at the end. That compact enclosure can recall a vessel with its space taken up.
usage_notes: The phrases 'pheno noru' and 'pheno lunei' mean a full bowl and a full moon. In a noun position, 'pheno' means fullness, as in 'pai pheno' (near fullness).

semantic_domains.physical: a vessel or form filled to relevant capacity or rounded by its contents
semantic_domains.spatial: the degree to which contents occupy a bounded space
